# trail-landing

> 

## Pages

- [Knowledge Infrastructure for the AI Era — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/): Trail is a knowledge infrastructure engine that turns your sources into a persistent, compounding Neuron-graph — maintained by an LLM on your behalf. Realizing Vannevar Bush
- [#accumulation — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/accumulation/): Content tagged accumulation.
- [#architecture — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/architecture/): Content tagged architecture.

- [#knowledge-infrastructure — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/knowledge-infrastructure/): Content tagged knowledge-infrastructure.
- [#knowledge-quality — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/knowledge-quality/): Content tagged knowledge-quality.
- [#lint — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/lint/): Content tagged lint.
- [#llm-wiki — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/llm-wiki/): Content tagged llm-wiki.
- [#luhmann — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/luhmann/): Content tagged luhmann.
- [#material-elasticity — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/material-elasticity/): Content tagged material-elasticity.
- [#memex — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/memex/): Content tagged memex.
- [#microfilm — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/microfilm/): Content tagged microfilm.
- [#neuroscience — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/neuroscience/): Content tagged neuroscience.
- [#nlp — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/nlp/): Content tagged nlp.
- [#notebooklm — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/notebooklm/): Content tagged notebooklm.
- [#policy — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/policy/): Content tagged policy.
- [#product — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/product/): Content tagged product.
- [#queue — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/queue/): Content tagged queue.
- [#rag — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/rag/): Content tagged rag.
- [#sampling — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/sampling/): Content tagged sampling.
- [#scalability — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/scalability/): Content tagged scalability.
- [#strategy — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/strategy/): Content tagged strategy.
- [#systems-design — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/systems-design/): Content tagged systems-design.
- [#vannevar-bush — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/vannevar-bush/): Content tagged vannevar-bush.
- [#zettelkasten — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/tags/zettelkasten/): Content tagged zettelkasten.
- [As We May Think](https://www.trailmem.com/the-1945-concept/): In July 1945, an American engineer named Vannevar Bush sketched a machine that would change how humanity thinks about knowledge. He called it the memex. Eighty-one years later, we are finally building it.
- [Dispatches — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/dispatches/): Releases, announcements, and observations from the broader knowledge-infrastructure landscape — what's shipping, what's shifting, what's worth watching.
- [Field Notes — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/field-notes/): Engineering diaries from inside the Trail build — pipelines, benchmarks, incidents, and the everyday craft of shipping a knowledge engine.
- [Compile-Time Knowledge: A Technical Argument Against RAG](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/how-trail-works/compile-time-knowledge-vs-rag/): RAG is a search engine wearing a language model as a hat. Trail compiles knowledge the way a brain consolidates memory — and the architectural difference shows up in latency, accuracy, provenance, and cost.
- [Does a Trail Get Smarter as It Grows?](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/how-trail-works/does-a-trail-get-smarter-as-it-grows/): More sources should mean a better knowledge base. In practice, every system hits a point where adding material starts to subtract. Here is what compounds, what buckles, and where Trail's architecture deliberately bends the curve.
- [How a Brain Actually Remembers: Why Trail Compiles Knowledge Instead of Searching For It](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/how-trail-works/how-a-brain-actually-remembers/): RAG treats knowledge like a filing cabinet. Brains do something fundamentally different — and so does trail. The architecture matters more than the model.
- [How Trail Works — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/how-trail-works/): Essays on Trail's architecture — ingest-time compilation, cross-referenced Neurons, and the design choices that make a knowledge graph compound instead of fragment.
- [Why Your Knowledge Base Should Compile, Not Search](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/how-trail-works/knowledge-that-compounds/): Most AI knowledge systems are sophisticated search engines that forget between queries. The organizations winning are building something different — and the architectural choice has direct consequences for cost, accuracy, and defensibility.
- [Three Filters on the Gate: How Trail's Curation Policy Borrows from the Structure of Your Mind](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/how-trail-works/three-filters-on-the-gate/): A knowledge system without filters isn't a brain — it's a garbage heap. Trail's trusted-pipeline, confidence-threshold, and no-contradictions trio isn't an arbitrary engineering choice. It mirrors a pattern two billion years of evolution converged on.
- [Work That Fits in a Night: How Trail Stops Caring About Corpus Size](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/how-trail-works/work-that-fits-in-a-night/): A compile-time knowledge system has to integrate new sources against everything it already knows. That integration cost grows with the corpus — until, one Tuesday, it stops finishing before the next night begins. Here is how Trail makes that cost bounded without going blind to the long tail.
- [Trails — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/): Essays, field notes, and dispatches from the trail knowledge engine.
- [Research — trail](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/research/): Deep dives into adjacent systems, benchmarks, and comparisons — from MemPalace and Mem0 to Karpathy's LLM Wiki and the long tail of RAG variants.
- [Niklas Luhmann's Zettelkasten Has Been Waiting Seventy Years for the LLM](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/research/niklas-luhmann-zettelkasten-llm-wiki/): Luhmann produced 90,000 cards and 500-plus books with a method that has worked for seven decades — but most of his time went into bookkeeping that does not directly contribute to thinking. LLM Neuron trails are the first plausible automation of exactly that bookkeeping. Trail is what it looks like as hosted infrastructure.
- [Scaling Trail from 200 to 100,000 Neurons: An Engineering Note](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/research/scaling-trail-from-200-to-100000-neurons/): A compile-time knowledge engine has three workloads that fight for compute — ingest, lint, and the curation queue. Each one breaks at a different corpus size, for different reasons, and requires a different fix. This is the long-form companion to Work That Fits in a Night: the full accounting of where Trail's bottlenecks are, when they hit, and what a deployment looks like at 200, 8,000, 25,000, and 100,000 Neurons.
- [Trail and NotebookLM: Same Ethos, Different Artifact](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/research/trail-and-notebooklm/): NotebookLM proved source-grounded ingest-time tools can ship to millions. Trail shares that ethos and diverges on one question: what the system leaves behind.
- [As We May Think](https://www.trailmem.com/trails/the-1945-concept/): In July 1945, an American engineer named Vannevar Bush sketched a machine that would change how humanity thinks about knowledge. He called it the memex. Eighty-one years later, we are finally building it.

Built with [webhouse.app](https://webhouse.app)
